summary: E-commerce and omnichannel DCs running SAP EWM can use Locus collaborative AMRs for pick-and-transport while EWM remains the system of record for inventory and work. Integration is typically a project: map workflows, agree task handoffs, then pilot in one zone before expanding.
architectureNotes: Orders and pick tasks originate in SAP EWM (or upstream ERP → EWM).
A fleet / orchestration layer receives eligible work and assigns Locus robots and associates.
Robots execute moves and confirmations flow back so EWM inventory and task status stay consistent.
Exceptions (blocked aisles, short picks) escalate to human workflows defined in ops SOPs — not only in software.
setupSteps: Confirm EWM release, warehouse number, and which process types (e.g. picking, replenishment) robots will support.
Document data contracts: task create/update, confirmations, and cancellation handling.
Stand up non-prod connectivity (API, middleware, or certified connector) and map IDs for locations and HU/totes.
Pilot in one aisle zone with clear success metrics (picks/hour, exception rate, confirmation latency).
Train floor leads on robot loading, traffic rules, and when to fall back to manual work.
Expand zones only after WMS confirmations and safety review are stable.

faqs.question: Does Locus plug into SAP EWM out of the box?
faqs.answer: Expect a scoped integration project. Some sites use vendor or SI connectors; others use middleware. Validate your EWM version and process types with Locus and your SAP partner before promising a timeline.

faqs.question: What breaks most often in WMS–AMR projects?
faqs.answer: Unclear exception paths, location master data mismatches, and pilots that skip confirmation latency testing. Treat mapping and ID hygiene as first-class work.

faqs.question: Should IT or operations own the pilot?
faqs.answer: Both. IT owns connectivity and security; operations owns zone design, staffing, and KPIs. Joint success criteria prevent a technically “live” integration that the floor does not trust.

faqs.question: How does this relate to PickTheRobot’s matcher?
faqs.answer: The matcher ranks robot type and acquisition fit from your facility answers. This page is research on WMS pairing — confirm commercial and technical fit with Locus and your SAP team.

summary: Mid-market WMS stacks can orchestrate Locus fleets when they can publish pick/transport tasks and accept confirmations. Many first pilots start with a light connector or middleware rather than deep customization.
architectureNotes: WMS (or WES) creates work for a zone or wave.
Middleware or fleet software translates tasks into robot missions.
Associates and robots collaborate; status returns to the WMS for inventory accuracy.
Reporting may live in fleet analytics until WMS dashboards catch up.
setupSteps: Inventory which WMS events you can emit (task assign, cancel, complete) and in what format (API, EDI, files).
Pick one workflow (e.g. pick-to-tote transport) with measurable volume.
Configure location and SKU/HU identifiers consistently across WMS and fleet maps.
Run a dry-run with simulated confirmations before robots move live product.
Define rollback: how associates finish work if the connector is down.
Review security: credentials, network segments, and who can change tasking rules.

faqs.question: Can we integrate without a full WES?
faqs.answer: Often yes for a narrow pilot. Broader multi-zone orchestration usually needs clearer task allocation — either in WMS/WES or a dedicated fleet layer.

faqs.question: How long do mid-market WMS connectors take?
faqs.answer: Simple file or REST pilots can be weeks; deeper real-time allocation is longer. Integration depth — not robot count — usually sets the critical path.

faqs.question: What should we ask Locus in the first call?
faqs.answer: Supported WMS patterns, reference sites at similar volume, pilot terms, and whether middleware is included or SI-led.

summary: MiR AMRs are often used for point-to-point and line-side transport. Pairing with a mid-market WMS usually means missions triggered by WMS events or MES/WMS signals rather than full goods-to-person allocation.
architectureNotes: WMS signals a move need (replenishment, buffer to pack, dock transfer).
Fleet manager or MiR software accepts a mission with source/destination.
Robot executes; confirmation updates the WMS task or inventory move.
Traffic and charging policies stay in the fleet layer; WMS stays authoritative for inventory.
setupSteps: List the 2–3 move types you want robots to own in phase one.
Confirm how the WMS can trigger those moves (API, PLC/MES bridge, or operator console).
Map physical locations to WMS location IDs and MiR maps.
Pilot one loop with clear SLAs for wait time and blocked-path handling.
Document who resets missions when Wi-Fi or maps fail.
Only then add concurrent missions or additional floors.

faqs.question: Is MiR a WMS replacement?
faqs.answer: No. MiR handles mobile execution; your WMS remains the inventory and order system of record.

faqs.question: Do we need AGV-style infrastructure?
faqs.answer: MiR AMRs navigate dynamically, but you still need reliable Wi-Fi, charging, and clear traffic rules. That is different from tape-guided AGV loops, not “zero facility work.”

faqs.question: When is MiR a better WMS partner than pick-assist fleets?
faqs.answer: When the bottleneck is repeatable transport between defined points rather than dense collaborative picking. Use the warehouse matcher to compare types for your pain point.

summary: OTTO autonomous vehicles often serve heavier material movement. WMS integration focuses on dispatching moves and confirming completions so dock, buffer, and production-adjacent flows stay synchronized with inventory.
architectureNotes: WMS or MES requests a material move with priority and destination.
Fleet software schedules OTTO vehicles and manages traffic.
Completion events update WMS inventory or production staging.
Safety zones and human traffic rules are enforced at the fleet/facility layer.
setupSteps: Define payload and path constraints (aisle width, intersections, pedestrian mix).
Agree which WMS transactions create robot-eligible moves.
Integrate dispatch and confirmation in a test environment first.
Pilot a single high-volume loop with spare-capacity planning.
Train material handlers on call points and exception tags.
Scale loops after cycle-time and safety reviews pass.

faqs.question: How is this different from AMR pick assist + WMS?
faqs.answer: OTTO-style vehicles target heavier or longer-haul moves. Pick-assist fleets target collaborative picking. Many sites use different robot types for different zones.

faqs.question: What WMS readiness do we need?
faqs.answer: At minimum, reliable location masters and a way to create/close move tasks. Deeper real-time optimization is optional for a first loop.

faqs.question: Where do I compare vendors after reading this?
faqs.answer: Open the OTTO vendor profile for strengths/limits, then run the warehouse matcher with your facility and pain-point answers.

summary: Autonomous scrubbers like Avidbots Neo work best when cleaning schedules and coverage reporting connect to facilities or janitorial ops tools — so robots handle routine passes and staff own restrooms, detailing, and exceptions.
architectureNotes: Ops platform (or robot cloud) holds schedules, maps, and site hierarchy.
Robots run planned coverage; completion and issue flags sync to ops dashboards.
Staff receive exception tickets (blocked areas, low battery, incomplete zones).
Building access and after-hours rules stay with facilities security processes.
setupSteps: Map floor plates and note no-go zones (stairs, glass, clutter).
Align cleaning windows with building access and alarm schedules.
Connect reporting so supervisors see completed vs skipped areas.
Pilot one floor with a clear handoff SOP for staff detailing.
Tune schedules after two weeks of real obstacle and traffic data.
Expand floors once mapping and exception response times are acceptable.

faqs.question: Do we need a full CMMS to start?
faqs.answer: No. Many sites start with the vendor’s cloud scheduling and export reports. A CMMS helps when you already manage work orders across many buildings.

faqs.question: Will the robot replace janitorial staff?
faqs.answer: Usually no. Robots cover open-floor routine work; staff still handle edges, restrooms, and guest-facing recovery. See our cleaning guides for buy vs RaaS framing.

faqs.question: How do I get a fit recommendation?
faqs.answer: Run the cleaning matcher with floor area, mess level, and budget preference, then review the Avidbots vendor profile.
